---
title: Balance Sheet
difficulty: 2
field: financial_statements
standard: J-GAAP
---

import { Callout } from '@/components/ui/callout'
import { Example } from '@/components/ui/example'
import { KeyConcept } from '@/components/ui/key-concept'

# Balance Sheet

## What is it — Intuitive explanation

A balance sheet is a financial statement that lists, at a **specific point in time**, what a company owns and where it obtained its funding. In English it is called a **Balance Sheet (B/S)**.

Simply put, it is "a snapshot of a company's financial position on a given day." If the income statement is a video showing "earnings over a period of time," then the balance sheet is a snapshot showing "the company's assets as of a specific date."

<Callout type="analogy">
💡 **Everyday analogy: your personal financial position**

When you think about "how wealthy am I as of today," you might reason like this:

- **What you own**: ¥100,000 in cash, ¥500,000 in a bank account, a car worth ¥1,000,000 (assets)
- **What you owe**: ¥800,000 in student loans (liabilities)
- **What's left for you**: ¥800,000 (net assets)

A balance sheet is exactly this kind of summary, done at the scale of a business.
</Callout>

---

## Why it matters — Its place in accounting and bookkeeping

### The "core" of financial statements

The ultimate goal of accounting is to communicate a company's financial position and operating results accurately to external stakeholders — investors, banks, business partners, and others. The balance sheet is the document that represents **financial position**, and together with the income statement and the cash flow statement it forms the three pillars of financial reporting.

### As a foundation for decision-making

When a bank evaluates a loan application, it needs to know "does this company have the capacity to repay?" When an investor considers buying shares, they need to confirm "does this company have real value?" The balance sheet serves as the **basis for assessing financial health** and is used by all stakeholders for exactly these purposes.

### As the final output of the bookkeeping cycle

Daily journal entries → ledger postings → trial balance preparation → closing adjustments (depreciation, provisions, accruals and deferrals) — the **final output** of this entire process is the financial statements, and the balance sheet sits at the center. The trial balance and closing adjustments studied as prerequisites all ultimately come together in this single statement.

---

## The core idea — The essence of the definition

### The balance sheet equation (the fundamental accounting equation)

The fundamental relationship underlying the balance sheet is as follows:

$$
\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Net Assets}
$$

This is called the **balance sheet equation** (or the accounting equation). The left side (assets) represents "the total value of everything the company owns," while the right side (liabilities + net assets) shows "how that value was financed." The two sides **always equal each other (always balance)** — which is where the name "Balance Sheet" comes from.

<KeyConcept>
Rearranged, the equation can also be expressed as:

$$
\text{Net Assets} = \text{Assets} - \text{Liabilities}
$$

Net assets represent "what remains for shareholders after all liabilities have been settled." This is also referred to as **equity**.
</KeyConcept>

### The three components

| Section | Meaning | Common examples |
|---------|---------|-----------------|
| **Assets** | Economic resources owned by the company | Cash, accounts receivable, buildings, machinery |
| **Liabilities** | Funds raised from others that carry a repayment obligation | Loans payable, accounts payable, bonds |
| **Net Assets** | Funds contributed by shareholders + accumulated profits to date | Share capital, retained earnings |

### Debit and credit placement

The balance sheet is presented as a table divided into left and right sides.

```
┌─────────────────┬─────────────────┐
│   Debit (Left)  │  Credit (Right) │
│                 │                 │
│    Assets       │   Liabilities   │
│                 │                 │
│                 │   Net Assets    │
└─────────────────┴─────────────────┘
     The totals on both sides always match
```

Assets are placed on the **left side (debit)**, and liabilities and net assets are placed on the **right side (credit)**. This is consistent with the debit/credit framework used in journal entries.

### Current and non-current classification of assets and liabilities (J-GAAP)

Under Japanese GAAP, assets and liabilities are further classified as **current or non-current**.

- **Current assets / Current liabilities**: Items expected to be converted to cash or settled within one year
- **Non-current assets / Non-current liabilities**: Items that extend beyond one year

This classification is known as the **"one-year rule"** and is useful for evaluating a company's short-term ability to meet its obligations.

<Callout type="info">
📌 **Key differences from IFRS**

Under IFRS, the current/non-current distinction is similarly required, but the **ordering of assets** may differ. Under J-GAAP, items with higher liquidity are presented at the top (left), whereas IFRS also permits a reversed order in which non-current assets are presented first. Additionally, IFRS uses the term **"Equity"** rather than "net assets."
</Callout>

---

## Concrete examples

<Example title="Example 1: Everyday life — A university student's financial position">

A university student named A decided to summarize their financial position "as of today" in balance sheet format.

```
【A's Balance Sheet (as of today)】Unit: ¥10,000

Assets                    Liabilities
──────────────────────────────────────
Cash             5        Student loan         80
Bank deposits   50        Credit card balance   5
Bicycle         10        ──────────────────────
                          Total liabilities     85

                          Net assets (residual) -20
                          ──────────────────────
Total assets    65        Total liabilities
                          & net assets          65
```

Net assets have turned negative. This means "if everything were liquidated right now, debts would remain" — a condition known as **insolvency (debt excess)**. If a company remains in this state, the risk of bankruptcy increases.

Equation check: $65 = 85 + (-20)$ ✓

</Example>

<Example title="Example 2: A small company on founding day — Visualizing fundraising">

Suppose Company B raises ¥3,000,000 from shareholders and ¥2,000,000 from a bank on its first day of incorporation, holding the full amount as cash.

```
【Company B Balance Sheet (founding day)】Unit: ¥10,000

(Assets)                      (Liabilities)
Current assets                Current liabilities
  Cash and deposits  500        Short-term loans    200
                              (Net Assets)
                                Share capital       300

Total assets         500      Total liabilities
                              & net assets         500
```

$$500 = 200 + 300$$

The equation holds. Looking at the right side, you can see exactly where the ¥5,000,000 in cash came from: ¥2,000,000 that must be repaid to the bank, and ¥3,000,000 contributed by shareholders.

</Example>

<Example title="Example 3: After one year of operations — Profits accumulate in net assets">

What would Company B's balance sheet look like after earning ¥1,000,000 in profit over the first year of operations?

```
【Company B Balance Sheet (after one year)】Unit: ¥10,000

(Assets)                      (Liabilities)
Current assets                Current liabilities
  Cash and deposits  600        Short-term loans    200
                              (Net Assets)
                                Share capital       300
                                Retained earnings   100

Total assets         600      Total liabilities
                              & net assets         600
```

The ¥1,000,000 profit is added to net assets as **retained earnings**. Cash (assets) also increases by ¥1,000,000, and the equation continues to hold. This is where you can see the connection: the net income calculated on the income statement carries over into net assets on the balance sheet.

$$600 = 200 + (300 + 100)$$

</Example>

---

## Connections — Relationships with other concepts

### Relationship with prerequisite knowledge

```
Journal entries & ledgers
         ↓
Trial balance
         ↓
Closing adjustments (depreciation, provisions, accruals & deferrals)
         ↓
【Balance Sheet】 ←→ Income Statement
```

- **Trial balance**: The raw material of the balance sheet. The balance column figures for each account in the trial balance are distributed into the corresponding line items on the balance sheet.
- **Closing adjustments**: The process of correcting trial balance figures to arrive at accurate period-end balances. For example, depreciation reduces the carrying amount of non-current assets, and recognizing provisions increases liabilities. Only once these adjustments are reflected is an accurate balance sheet complete.

### Relationship with the income statement

The balance sheet and income statement are not independent documents — they are linked through **net income**.

$$
\text{Closing net assets} = \text{Opening net assets} + \text{Net income for the period} - \text{Dividends, etc.}
$$

Net income calculated on the income statement is added to **retained earnings** on the balance sheet, creating the linkage between the two statements.

### Connections to more advanced concepts

| Advanced concept | Relationship to the balance sheet |
|-----------------|----------------------------------|
| Cash flow statement | Explains changes in cash and cash equivalents (an asset) |
| Financial ratio analysis | Management analysis using B/S figures, e.g., current ratio, equity ratio |
| Consolidated financial statements | A "group-wide" B/S combining parent and subsidiary companies |
| Impairment accounting | Reduces the carrying amount of non-current assets, reflected on the B/S |

---

## Applications to other fields

### Business management and corporate strategy

Managers use the balance sheet to measure their company's capital efficiency. For example, **ROE (Return on Equity)** divides net income by net assets, drawing on both the balance sheet and the income statement. It measures "how much profit is being generated relative to the equity invested" and is a central metric in corporate strategy.

$$
ROE = \frac{\text{Net income}}{\text{Net assets}} \times 100 \ (\%)
$$

### Banking and credit management

When banks and suppliers assess the creditworthiness of a counterparty, the balance sheet is an indispensable tool. By examining the **equity ratio** (net assets ÷ total assets) and the **debt-to-equity ratio** (liabilities ÷ net assets), they can objectively assess "can this company repay its debts?" and "how high is its bankruptcy risk?"

### Tax and legal matters

Under the Companies Act in Japan, the concept of net assets (distributable amount) is used to calculate the maximum permissible dividend. In tax filings, the period-end balances on the balance sheet also form the underlying data for tax return documents. For small and medium-sized enterprises, **attaching a balance sheet to the tax return is a legal requirement**, making accurate preparation a legal obligation.

### M&A and business valuation

When acquiring a company, the net asset value shown on the balance sheet (the **book value method**) is sometimes used as the basis for calculating the acquisition price. The difference between that figure and intangible value not appearing on the balance sheet — such as brand strength and human capital — is recorded as "goodwill," which is also an important topic in M&A contexts.

<Callout type="summary">
📝 **Summary**

- The balance sheet is a snapshot showing a company's financial position at a specific point in time
- Its core is the equation $\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Net Assets}$
- It is prepared through a trial balance and closing adjustments, and is linked to the income statement via net income
- It is the foundation of accounting, used in investment, lending, management, legal matters, and beyond
</Callout>

terms.definition: A financial statement that lists the status of a company's assets, liabilities, and net assets at a specific point in time. Also known in English as a Balance Sheet (B/S).
A collective term for the economic resources held by a company. Includes cash, accounts receivable, buildings, machinery, and more; displayed on the left side (debit side) of the balance sheet.
Funds procured from external parties that carry a repayment obligation. Includes loans payable, accounts payable, and bonds; displayed on the right side (credit side) of the balance sheet.
The residual amount after subtracting liabilities from assets, representing the sum of funds raised from shareholders and accumulated profits. Also referred to as shareholders' equity.
The fundamental identity in accounting expressed as "Assets = Liabilities + Net Assets." The left and right sides of the balance sheet always balance.
Assets expected to be converted into cash within one year. Includes cash, accounts receivable, and inventories, classified according to the one-year rule.
Assets held or used for more than one year. Includes buildings, machinery, and land, forming the long-term operational foundation of a business.
The portion of cumulative profits accumulated by a company over time that has not been distributed externally. Increases as net income from the income statement is added.
The final profit for a given accounting period. Calculated on the income statement and added to retained earnings on the balance sheet, linking the two financial statements.

A ratio calculated by dividing net assets by total assets, serving as an indicator of a company's financial stability. A higher ratio indicates lower dependence on borrowing and is considered more financially sound.
A metric calculated by dividing net income by net assets, indicating how efficiently shareholder equity is being converted into profit. It serves as a measure of management efficiency.
A condition in which total liabilities exceed total assets, resulting in negative net assets. This situation is considered to heighten the risk of bankruptcy.

The criterion used to classify assets and liabilities as current or non-current, based on whether they fall within one year or not. It is the basic rule for financial statement presentation under J-GAAP.

The difference between the acquisition price in an M&A transaction and the book value of net assets of the acquired company. It reflects intangible value not recorded on the balance sheet, such as brand equity and human capital.

diagrams.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 420" width="600" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="system-ui, sans-serif">

  <!-- Title -->
  <text x="300" y="32" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Balance Sheet Structure</text>

  <!-- Equation -->
  <text x="300" y="60" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="currentColor">Assets ＝ Liabilities ＋ Net Assets</text>

  <!-- Main Balance Sheet Frame -->
  <!-- Left side: Assets -->
  <rect x="40" y="80" width="240" height="300" rx="8" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- Right side: Liabilities + Equity -->
  <rect x="320" y="80" width="240" height="300" rx="8" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>

  <!-- Center divider label -->
  <text x="300" y="245" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor" transform="rotate(0)">＝</text>

  <!-- LEFT SIDE HEADER: Debit -->
  <rect x="40" y="80" width="240" height="36" rx="8" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.15" stroke="none"/>
  <rect x="40" y="104" width="240" height="12" rx="0" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.15" stroke="none"/>
  <text x="160" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Debit (Left Side)</text>

  <!-- RIGHT SIDE HEADER: Credit -->
  <rect x="320" y="80" width="240" height="36" rx="8" fill="#ef4444" fill-opacity="0.15" stroke="none"/>
  <rect x="320" y="104" width="240" height="12" rx="0" fill="#ef4444" fill-opacity="0.15" stroke="none"/>
  <text x="440" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#ef4444">Credit (Right Side)</text>

  <!-- ASSET SECTION -->
  <text x="160" y="135" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Assets</text>

  <!-- Current Assets block -->
  <rect x="55" y="148" width="210" height="90" rx="6" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <text x="160" y="167" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Current Assets</text>
  <text x="160" y="184" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Cash & Deposits</text>
  <text x="160" y="199" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Accounts Receivable</text>
  <text x="160" y="214" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Inventories</text>
  <text x="245" y="200" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#3b82f6">Within 1 Year</text>

  <!-- Fixed Assets block -->
  <rect x="55" y="250" width="210" height="90" rx="6" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.04" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <text x="160" y="269" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Non-Current Assets</text>
  <text x="160" y="286" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Buildings & Machinery</text>
  <text x="160" y="301" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Land</text>
  <text x="160" y="316" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Intangible Assets</text>
  <text x="245" y="302" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#3b82f6">Over 1 Year</text>

  <!-- Asset total -->
  <line x1="55" y1="354" x2="265" y2="354" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="85" y="370" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Total Assets</text>
  <text x="255" y="370" text-anchor="end" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">XXX</text>

  <!-- LIABILITY SECTION -->
  <!-- Current Liabilities block -->
  <text x="440" y="135" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#ef4444">Liabilities</text>

  <rect x="335" y="148" width="210" height="80" rx="6" fill="#ef4444" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <text x="440" y="167" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#ef4444">Current Liabilities</text>
  <text x="440" y="184" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Accounts Payable</text>
  <text x="440" y="199" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Short-term Borrowings</text>
  <text x="525" y="190" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">Within 1 Year</text>

  <!-- Non-Current Liabilities block -->
  <rect x="335" y="238" width="210" height="60" rx="6" fill="#ef4444" fill-opacity="0.04" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <text x="440" y="257" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#ef4444">Non-Current Liabilities</text>
  <text x="440" y="274" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Long-term Loans & Bonds</text>
  <text x="525" y="263" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">Over 1 Year</text>

  <!-- Net Assets section -->
  <rect x="335" y="308" width="210" height="45" rx="6" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="440" y="327" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#22c55e">Net Assets (Equity)</text>
  <text x="440" y="344" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Capital & Retained Earnings</text>

  <!-- Right total -->
  <line x1="335" y1="365" x2="545" y2="365" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="365" y="380" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Total Liabilities & Net Assets</text>
  <text x="535" y="380" text-anchor="end" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">XXX</text>

  <!-- Center balance symbol -->
  <text x="300" y="235" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" fill="currentColor" font-weight="bold">⇌</text>

  <!-- Balance annotation -->
  <text x="300" y="395" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Both sides always balance (are equal)</text>

  <!-- 1-year rule annotation -->
  <rect x="100" y="243" width="60" height="14" rx="3" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05"/>
  <text x="130" y="253" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8.5" fill="currentColor">Classified by 1-year rule</text>
  <rect x="380" y="230" width="60" height="14" rx="3" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05"/>
  <text x="410" y="240" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8.5" fill="currentColor">Classified by 1-year rule</text>

</svg>
